2025 · arXiv · Post-training & RL

Does Reinforcement Learning Really Incentivize Reasoning Capacity in LLMs Beyond the Base Model?

A systematic pass@k study argues that current RLVR doesn't teach models to reason — it teaches them to sample, more reliably, solutions the base model could already reach. At large k the base model matches or beats its RLVR-trained version, RLVR's reasoning paths already live in the base distribution, and only distillation moves the boundary.

♪ listen — audio episode
3-voice deep dive · mp3 ↗ · rss feed ↗
Yang Yue, Zhiqi Chen, Rui Lu, Andrew Zhao, Zhaokai Wang, Yang Yue, Shiji Song, Gao Huang · Tsinghua (LeapLab) / SJTU
venue arXiv citations 852 topic Post-training & RL source ↗ listen ♪
// citation count as of 2026-06-20
// the gap

What problem is this solving?

The recent leap in reasoning LLMs — OpenAI-o1, DeepSeek-R1, Kimi-1.5 — is widely credited to Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR): start from a pretrained base model and optimize it against an automatic, binary reward (final answer matches the key, or the code passes unit tests). The implicit story is that RLVR works the way classic RL works in Go or Atari, where agents explore and discover strategies that exceed where they started. Under that story, RLVR is a path to continuously self-improving models whose reasoning surpasses the base model's.

But the evidence for "new capability" rests almost entirely on average-case metrics — greedy decoding or the mean of nucleus samples, which is just pass@1. Those metrics reward producing the right answer reliably, and that cannot distinguish two very different things: a model that can now solve something it genuinely could not before, versus a model that is simply more likely to surface an answer it could already produce on a lucky draw. Nobody had directly probed the capability boundary: is there any problem the RLVR model can solve that the base model cannot, at any sampling budget?

This paper takes that question literally and answers it empirically, across model families, RL algorithms, and math / code / visual-reasoning benchmarks.

// how it works

The method

The instrument is pass@k at large k. For a problem, sample k outputs; pass@k = 1 if any of them passes the verifier. Averaged over a dataset, it is the fraction of problems the model can solve within k attempts. As k grows, this stops measuring average behavior and starts tracing the model's reasoning boundary — its potential, not its luck. The logic is simple: if RLVR truly adds capability, the RL model's pass@k curve should sit above the base model's at every k. They use the unbiased, low-variance estimator (sample n ≥ k per problem, count c correct):

pass@k = 𝔼x∼𝒟 [ 1 − C(n − c, k)C(n, k) ] // unbiased pass@k estimator (Chen et al., 2021); n is the largest k on the curve — 128, 256, or 1024

Three design choices make the comparison hard to argue with. (1) Not best-of-N or majority voting — those measure whether a selector picks a correct sample, and can throw away a correct answer the model did produce; pass@k credits the model with a problem as long as a correct sample exists. (2) No few-shot prompts for base models — base and RL models get the identical zero-shot prompt, so in-context examples can't confound the result (base models do produce unformatted junk early, but with enough samples they format and solve). (3) Guard against guessing — for code, unit tests make a lucky pass implausible; for math, where a wrong chain can stumble onto the right number, the authors manually inspect the chains-of-thought of correct answers on the hardest solvable problems.

The experimental matrix is deliberately broad: three domains (math, code, vision), multiple families (Qwen2.5-7B/14B/32B-Base, Qwen2.5-Math-7B, LLaMA-3.1-8B, Qwen2.5-VL-7B), and many RL recipes — released models from SimpleRLZoo, Oat-Zero, DAPO, Code-R1, DeepCoder and EasyR1, plus a clean re-implementation on the VeRL framework of six algorithms (PPO, GRPO, Reinforce++, RLOO, ReMax, DAPO) for an apples-to-apples comparison. Two further probes pin down where RLVR's outputs come from: perplexity (is an RL response something the base model was already likely to generate?) and a Sampling Efficiency Gap that measures how far an RL model sits below the base-model ceiling:

ΔSE = pass@kbase(k=256) − pass@1RL // distance from the base model's coverage ceiling; lower is better
// what they found

The results

The pass@k curves cross, everywhere. At small k the RL model wins (this is the familiar "RL improves performance"). But as k grows the base model catches up and overtakes it — on every math benchmark and family. On Minerva with a 32B model the base model leads by ~9% at k=128; the strongest AIME24 RL models (Oat-Zero, DAPO) start nearly 30% above the base yet are still surpassed once k is large enough.

The same crossover appears in code and vision, where guessing is implausible (passing all unit tests by luck is essentially impossible). On MathVision the base model clearly overtakes the RL model at high k.

It isn't a guessing artifact. Manual CoT audits on the hardest GSM8K problems (average accuracy 0–5%): the base model solved 25 such problems with 24 containing a genuinely valid chain; the RL model solved 25 with 23 valid. On AIME24's hardest, 5 of 6 base solutions and 4 of 6 RL solutions had a valid chain. And the solvable sets are nearly nested: the problems an RL model can solve are almost a subset of what the base can solve.

Solvable by…AIME24 (k=1024)MATH500 (k=128)
Base ✓  ·  RL ✓63.3%92.4%
Base ✓  ·  RL ✗  (base-only)13.3%3.6%
Base ✗  ·  RL ✓  (RL-only)0.0%1.0%
Base ✗  ·  RL ✗23.3%3.0%

// Table 2: base-only solvable problems vastly outnumber RL-only ones (13.3% vs 0.0% on AIME24).

Why? Because RLVR outputs are already in the base model's distribution. Measuring the perplexity of RL responses under the base model, they fall in the low-perplexity tail of the base model's own outputs — i.e. exactly the things the base was already most likely to say — while OpenAI-o1's responses score far higher (genuinely out-of-distribution for the base). As RL training proceeds, that perplexity drops further: RLVR sharpens the base prior rather than reaching beyond it.

Training makes the boundary worse, not better: as GRPO steps accumulate, pass@1 climbs from 26.1 to 42.5 while pass@256 steadily declines. And the algorithm barely matters — across the six clean re-implementations the sampling-efficiency gap ΔSE ranges only from 43.9 (GRPO) to 42.6 (RLOO) in-domain and stays above 40 points everywhere. All six recipes are similarly far from the base-model ceiling.

The pattern even holds for a near-frontier model: Magistral-Medium (pure RL on Mistral-Medium-3) solves ≈7 more AIME24 and ≈8 more AIME25 problems than its base at k=1, but the gap vanishes as k grows. The one intervention that genuinely expands the boundary is distillation: DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B sits consistently above its base at every k, because it imports new reasoning patterns from a stronger teacher — something RLVR does not do.

// does it hold up?

The eval read

The result is striking and the methodology is unusually careful for a "negative result" paper — within-model comparisons, six algorithms on one framework, manual CoT audits to defuse the guessing objection. But the scope deserves to be read precisely, because the title invites an over-reading. The claim is about current RLVR — binary outcome rewards, single-turn, modest compute — not about RL in principle, and the authors say so explicitly.

The sharpest caveat is the base-model zoo. It is Qwen-dominated, and Qwen base models are unusually "RL-primed" — already fluent in these benchmark formats — so a base model that recovers solutions at high k may be a property of that family. LLaMA-3.1-8B is the lone non-Qwen family and is comparatively weak. A parallel line of work ("spurious rewards") has since shown that RLVR conclusions can flip across base families, which makes family coverage the first thing to stress-test. Second, pass@k at large k is itself a proxy for the boundary: it credits any correct sample, so it can flatter a base model that stumbles onto answers — the CoT-validity audits are reassuring but cover only a few dozen hard problems, and one AIME case was set aside as ambiguous. Third, the compute ceiling is real: rollouts capped at 8 (probed to 32), 8,192-token generations, ~450 GRPO steps. The authors leave "can scaled RLVR eventually surpass base?" open, and couldn't cleanly isolate frontier R1-Zero / Qwen3 / o1.

baselines

Fair and current — base vs. its own RLVR checkpoint, six modern algorithms re-implemented on one framework, plus released Oat-Zero / DAPO / DeepCoder. But the base zoo is Qwen-heavy; LLaMA-3.1-8B is the only outsider.

contamination

Largely sidestepped: the comparison is within-model, so any benchmark leakage hits base and RL equally. LiveCodeBench v5 is time-windowed (Aug 2024–Jan 2025); older math sets may be partly seen, but that doesn't move the relative crossover.

what's missed

Large-k pass@k rewards lucky correct samples; the manual CoT audits are small. The story is outcome-reward, single-turn RLVR at small compute — not multi-turn / agentic or process-reward RL, and not frontier scale.

would raise confidence

Non-Qwen base families at scale; larger CoT-validity audits; a cleanly isolated frontier from-base RLVR run; and evidence on whether much larger rollout / compute budgets ever push RL pass@k above base.

// bottom line

The headline is not "RL doesn't work" — RLVR reliably lifts pass@1, which is what actually ships. It is that, as practiced today, RLVR redistributes probability inside the base model's existing reasoning rather than expanding it: it sharpens, it doesn't discover. Distillation can move the boundary; current RLVR mostly moves the odds of sampling. The honest open question the paper leaves is whether that's a property of reinforcement learning itself, or just of today's exploration-poor, outcome-only recipes at modest compute.

// takeaways

What to remember

  1. pass@1 up, large-k pass@k flat-or-down. RLVR makes correct answers more likely to be sampled, but base models match or beat their RLVR versions once you sample enough — base leads by ~9% at k=128 on Minerva-32B, and the curves cross on every family and benchmark.
  2. RLVR reasoning is in-distribution for the base. RL responses sit in the low-perplexity tail of the base model, the RL-solvable set is nearly a subset of the base's (13.3% base-only vs 0.0% RL-only on AIME24), and more training narrows coverage (pass@1 26.1→42.5 while pass@256 falls).
  3. Algorithm choice barely matters; distillation does. PPO / GRPO / Reinforce++ / RLOO / ReMax / DAPO all leave ΔSE above 40 points, while distillation from a stronger teacher is the one intervention that lifts the reasoning boundary beyond the base model.
